title: Contract Types
summary: The main procurement contract families and how each one shifts cost risk between buyer and seller.

sections.heading: Why contract type matters
sections.body:
The contract type is a deliberate risk decision: it sets who carries the cost risk if the work turns out to be more expensive than expected. Choosing the right type is mostly about how well the scope is defined.

- Well-defined, stable scope favours fixed price, because the seller can price the work with confidence.
- Uncertain or evolving scope favours cost-reimbursable or time & materials, because neither side can yet price the full job.

Procurement spans both the Process and Business Environment domains of the current ECO, so expect questions that mix the contracting mechanics with stakeholder and compliance considerations.
sections.tip: Match the contract to the clarity of scope: clear scope to fixed price, fuzzy scope to cost-reimbursable or T&M.

sections.heading: Fixed-Price (FP) contracts
sections.body:
Under fixed price the buyer's risk is LOW and the seller's risk is HIGH: the seller is locked to a price and absorbs the consequences of getting the estimate wrong. Best used when scope is well defined.

- Firm Fixed Price (FFP) — a single set price; the seller absorbs any overrun in full.
- Fixed Price Incentive Fee (FPIF) — a set price plus an incentive for meeting cost, schedule or performance targets; buyer and seller share savings and overruns up to a price ceiling.
- Fixed Price with Economic Price Adjustment (FP-EPA) — used for long-term contracts; the price can adjust for defined external economics such as inflation or commodity rates.
sections.tip: FFP = seller carries all the cost risk; it is the buyer's safest, most predictable option.

sections.heading: Cost-Reimbursable (CR) contracts
sections.body:
Under cost-reimbursable the buyer pays the seller's allowable costs PLUS a fee. The buyer's risk is HIGHER because the final cost is uncertain until the work is done. Suited to scope that cannot yet be fully defined.

- Cost Plus Fixed Fee (CPFF) — costs plus a fixed fee that does not change with performance.
- Cost Plus Incentive Fee (CPIF) — costs plus a fee that varies with performance against targets, using an agreed sharing ratio.
- Cost Plus Award Fee (CPAF) — costs plus an award fee granted on the buyer's subjective satisfaction with performance.
sections.tip: Cost Plus means the buyer reimburses costs and then adds a fee; the buyer owns the cost uncertainty.

sections.heading: Time & Materials (T&M)
sections.body:
T&M is a hybrid of fixed-price and cost-reimbursable: the buyer pays an agreed rate per hour or unit, plus the cost of materials. It is well suited to staff augmentation or to work that must start before scope is fully clear.

Risk sits between FP and CR. Because there is no fixed total, cap the buyer's exposure with a not-to-exceed (NTE) ceiling and time limits, so an open-ended arrangement cannot run away.
sections.tip: T&M with no NTE cap is the trap answer; always bound it with a not-to-exceed clause.

sections.heading: The risk spectrum and Point of Total Assumption
sections.body:
The families sit on a single risk spectrum. The seller bears the most cost risk under FFP; the buyer bears the most under CPFF; FPIF, FP-EPA, CPIF, CPAF and T&M fall in between.

In an FPIF contract the Point of Total Assumption (PTA) is the cost level above which the SELLER bears all additional cost: above the PTA the cost-sharing stops and every extra pound is the seller's.
sections.tip: Above the PTA, the seller absorbs 100% of further cost; below it, buyer and seller share.

figure.caption: Risk crosses over: seller bears most under FFP, buyer most under CPFF.

source: PM Cram study notes — reworded for the current ECO-based PMP exam.
